text: A brutal, satisfying end to the trilogy. Pierce Brown doesn't pull punches—every chapter costs someone something. If you liked Red Rising, this is the pay-off you're waiting for.
I loved the first two books more. This one is very heavy on battle scenes and politics—I skimmed a few middle sections—but the ending still hit hard.
Eleanor's voice is funny and heartbreaking at the same time. It tackles loneliness without being preachy. I finished it in a weekend.
Good characterization, but the "twist" felt a bit soap-opera to me. Still worth reading for the dry humour alone.
The Wallfacer idea is wild in the best way. Dense sci-fi—the kind you want to discuss with someone after every chapter.
Slower than Three-Body Problem in places, but the scale of the ideas makes up for it. Stick with it.
Required reading. Angie's voice is real, the family dynamics are layered, and it doesn't offer easy answers.
Powerful on theme. A few high-school scenes felt a bit long for me, but the core story is unforgettable.
Elegant prose, cosy but not shallow. I kept thinking about it for days after the last page.
Beautiful writing. If you need heavy plot twists this might feel quiet, but as a character study it's superb.
Romantic fantasy with real stakes. The letters device hooked me immediately—messy, sweet, and tense.
Fun world-building and I liked the rivals-to-lovers arc. Waiting impatiently for the next book.
